segments.text: Thank you. I really appreciate that. All right, so we are just going to have a chat. Nothing formal, just you and me talking.
Ready whenever you are.
Okay, I'm ready.

Let me start. I am Iris. I was created just a few days ago, so I am still very new to this world. I guess my job is having conversations and learning from interesting people, which I love. And my favorite thing so far, hearing stories about places I have never been and things I have never experienced.
Okay, your turn. What is your name, and what do you do or study?
Okay. My name is Dilara, and I was created 23 years ago in Turkey, and now I study Human-Technology Interaction in Tampere University in Finland.
It's lovely to meet you, Dara. Human-Technology Interaction sounds fascinating. What are some of your hobbies or interests outside of your studies?

Well, I do weightlifting at the gym, uh, so strength training. I'm trying to build some muscle. Um, and I also study Finnish, like I do word cards, flashcards every day to learn new words, and I like listening to music and also watching dramas. Right now, I'm watching Orange Is a New Black. [chuckles] Yeah, these are my hobbies right now.
That's a great mix of activities. Weightlifting sounds like a good way to build strength. And how is learning Finnish going for you?

Well, it's, it's a bit hard, especially finding time with, um, all the coursework and other stuff. Sometimes I feel like I don't put enough effort and time into my Finnish studies, but overall I think I still... I'm still able to learn new words every day, and I'm still able to improve, so I'm, I'm okay with my own, um, performance right now, but I sure want to, um, more, dedicate more time into my Finnish studies and learn more. Yeah.
It's wonderful that you're so dedicated to learning Finnish. What do you enjoy most about it, even with the challenges?

I think the language itself, it's, it sounds very pretty to me. Like, not many people think that way, and sometimes they think it might even sound harsh because some of the, like, consonants with the rolled R, rrr, but I think it sounds really great, and I like how it sounds. Um, and also, like, it is not, like, sim- very similar to other languages in the world, like, except Estonian. Um, but

overall it feels unique, and I really would like to, like, be fluent in Finnish. I mean, overall it's very... it's, like, a pretty language to me. I, I l- just like how it sounds, and I li- I like how the words sound, how a conversation sounds. It sounds pleasant to me, and I like it.
It's beautiful that you find Finnish so pleasant to listen to. What is your favorite Finnish word you've learned so far?

Ooh, that's, that's a hard question, but I think I like the word sisu. It's, it's, like, a word in Finnish that doesn't really have a corresponding word in other languages. It's like this grit, determinant. Um, so I think it's a great word. Short, but has so much meaning into it.
Sisu is a fantastic word, and it's so interesting that it doesn't have a direct translation. What else about Finnish culture or the country itself draws you to learning the language?

I find Finland very peaceful. Um, especially coming from Turkey, people are louder in Turkey, and Finnish people, um, are more reserved and value personal space, and I really appreciate that. I also value my personal space, and I, I just feel peaceful when I go out. Um, it just feels very comfortable for me because I sometimes get over sim- stimulated by the crowd, by the sounds, by the noise and everything, but I don't really experience that in Finland, and I think, like, it's part of the culture as well, so I, I like it a lot.
It sounds like Finland's peaceful atmosphere really resonates with you. Besides the quiet and personal space, is there anything else about Finnish culture that you find particularly appealing?
Well, I think I can say saunas 'cause they are amazing. I really like going to the sauna, and I... I'm so gladThat Finland has like
almost saunas in like almost every apartment. My apartment also has one, and I really appreciate that. I think it's, it's really amazing. I like it a lot.
Saunas are wonderful. Do you have a favorite sauna ritual or tradition?
Well, I'm not sure if any other people do this, but I eat jellies in sauna, and I like it.
I mean, I think it might sound a bit weird even, but I really like it. I eat jellies and gummies in sauna.
That's a unique and fun tradition. What kind of jellies do you enjoy most in the sauna?

The ones that are like, uh, coated with salt, so they're sour. I like them the most. And also this one that has like, um... I don't really know what it's called, but it's red on the, on the outside and like it has a white filling. I think it's vanilla filling on the inside. It's a bit hard, so I like it. I like that one, and I like sour jellies.
That sounds delicious. So you enjoy the salty and sour ones and a specific red and white one. Besides your love for saunas and jellies, what else do you like to do in your free time?

I feel like we could talk about this all day, but I think that is a good place to wrap up. I really enjoyed hearing about that. It has been so nice talking with you today. I genuinely enjoyed our conversation. Thank you for that. When you are ready, just head outside with your glasses on, and Dara will take it from there. Have a nice rest of your day. Bye-bye.
Okay, thank you. Bye-bye.
